verses.flow: Now it came about in the days when the judges governed, that there was a famine in the land. And a man from Bethlehem in Judah went to sojourn in the land of Moab, he and his wife and his two sons.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "In the days when the judges governed" (bimey shephot hashophetim) — places Ruth in the period of Judges, a time of spiritual decline and moral chaos (Judg. 21:25). The famine echoes God's covenant warnings (Deut. 28:23-24). Bethlehem (beth lechem = "house of bread") having no bread is deeply ironic.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: And the name of the man was Elimelech, and the name of his wife was Naomi, and the names of his two sons were Mahlon and Chilion, Ephrathites of Bethlehem in Judah. And they came to the land of Moab and remained there.
verses.note: HEBREW: Elimelech ('eli-melekh) = "my God is King." Naomi (na'omi) = "pleasant, delightful." Mahlon = "sickness" and Chilion = "wasting, destruction" — their names foreshadow their deaths. Ephrathites links them to the clan of Ephrath, the ancient name for Bethlehem (Gen. 35:19).

verses.flow: Then both Mahlon and Chilion also died, and the woman was left without her two children and without her husband.

verses.flow: Then she arose with her daughters-in-law that she might return from the land of Moab, for she had heard in the land of Moab that the LORD had visited His people by giving them food.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The LORD had visited" (paqad YHWH) — paqad carries the sense of divine intervention, a purposeful act of attention. The same word describes God's visitation to Sarah (Gen. 21:1) and Israel in Egypt (Exod. 4:31).

verses.flow: So she departed from the place where she was, and her two daughters-in-law with her; and they went on the way to return to the land of Judah.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, "Go, return each of you to her mother's house. May the LORD deal kindly with you, as you have dealt with the dead and with me.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Deal kindly" (ya'aseh YHWH 'immakhem chesed) — first occurrence of chesed in Ruth. Chesed = covenant loyalty, steadfast love, lovingkindness. This word drives the entire book. Naomi prays the LORD would show the same chesed to them that they showed to the dead.

verses.flow: May the LORD grant that you may find rest, each in the house of her husband." Then she kissed them, and they lifted up their voices and wept.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem plur

verses.flow: And they said to her, "No, but we will return with you to your people."

verses.flow: But Naomi said, "Return, my daughters. Why should you go with me? Have I yet sons in my womb that they may be your husbands?
Return, my daughters! Go, for I am too old to have a husband. Even if I said I have hope, even if I should have a husband tonight and also bear sons,

would you therefore wait until they were grown? Would you therefore refrain from marrying? No, my daughters; for it is more bitter for me than for you, for the hand of the LORD has gone forth against me."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The hand of the LORD has gone forth against me" — Naomi interprets her suffering as divine judgment. Her theology is incomplete but honest. She acknowledges God's sovereignty even in suffering, a raw faith that will be redeemed by the end of the book.

verses.flow: And they lifted up their voices and wept again. And Orpah kissed her mother-in-law, but Ruth clung to her.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruth clung to her" (Ruth davqah bah) — davaq is the same verb used in Gen. 2:24 for a man "cleaving" to his wife. It denotes permanent, covenantal bonding. Orpah's kiss is farewell; Ruth's clinging is covenant.

verses.flow: But Ruth said, "Do not urge me to leave you or to turn back from following you; for where you go, I will go, and where you lodge, I will lodge. Your people shall be my people, and your God, my God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Ruth's confession — "your people shall be my people, and your God, my God" — is one of the greatest statements of covenant loyalty (chesed) in all of scripture. A Moabite woman voluntarily binds herself to the God of Israel. This is a conversion statement: Ruth forsakes Chemosh for YHWH. She uses covenant language that mirrors marriage and adoption formulas in the ancient Near East.

verses.flow: Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. Thus may the LORD do to me, and more also, if anything but death separates me from you."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Thus may the LORD do to me, and more also" (koh ya'aseh YHWH li wekhoh yosif) — a self-imprecatory oath formula. Ruth invokes YHWH by name, confirming her conversion. She binds herself to Israel's God even unto death and burial — a total, irrevocable commitment.

verses.flow: And she said to them, "Do not call me Naomi; call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.
verses.note: HEBREW: Naomi (na'omi = "pleasant") asks to be called Mara (mara = "bitter"). The Almighty (Shaddai) — she uses this ancient patriarchal name for God, emphasizing His power over her fate. Her bitterness is the low point from which God's redemption will lift her.

verses.flow: I went out full, but the LORD has brought me back empty. Why do you call me Naomi, since the LORD has witnessed against me and the Almighty has afflicted me?"

verses.flow: So Naomi returned, and with her Ruth the Moabitess, her daughter-in-law, who returned from the land of Moab. And they came to Bethlehem at the beginning of barley harvest.
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Ruth arrives in Bethlehem "at the beginning of barley harvest" — the season of Passover and Firstfruits. This Moabite woman entering God's provision in Bethlehem ("house of bread") at harvest time foreshadows the inclusion of Gentiles in God's redemptive plan. Bethlehem will later be the birthplace of David and of Christ (Mic. 5:2; Matt. 2:1).

verses.flow: Now Naomi had a kinsman of her husband, a man of great wealth, of the family of Elimelech, whose name was Boaz.
verses.note: HEBREW: "A kinsman" (meyudda') and "a man of great wealth" ('ish gibbor chayil) — gibbor chayil means a mighty man of valor/substance, denoting both wealth and character. Boaz's name (bo'az) likely means "in him is strength." He is introduced before he appears, as the answer to the problem before it is stated.

verses.flow: And Ruth the Moabitess said to Naomi, "Please let me go to the field and glean among the ears of grain after one in whose sight I may find favor." And she said to her, "Go, my daughter."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The gleaning laws of Lev. 19:9-10 and Deut. 24:19-22 specifically protect the poor, the stranger, the orphan, and the widow — Ruth is all of these at once. God's law makes provision for the marginalized through the generosity of the landowner.

verses.flow: Then she fell on her face, bowing to the ground, and said to him, "Why have I found favor in your eyes that you should take notice of me, since I am a foreigner?"
verses.note: HEBREW: "A foreigner" (nokhriyyah) — Ruth uses the strongest term for outsider, not ger (resident alien) but nokhri (true foreigner with no legal standing). She is acutely aware of her status. Yet God's law specifically includes her.

verses.flow: And Boaz answered and said to her, "All that you have done for your mother-in-law after the death of your husband has been fully reported to me, and how you left your father and your mother and the land of your birth, and came to a people that you did not previously know.

verses.flow: May the LORD reward your work, and your wages be full from the LORD, the God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take refuge."
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "Under whose wings (kanaf) you have come to take refuge" — kanaf means wing or edge of a garment. The image is of a bird sheltering its young (Ps. 91:4; Matt. 23:37). In 3:9 Ruth will ask Boaz to spread his kanaf over her — the human outworking of divine refuge. Boaz becomes the instrument of the very blessing he pronounced. This is a type of Christ who is both the one who blesses and the blessing itself.

verses.flow: And Boaz said to her at mealtime, "Come here, and eat of the bread and dip your piece in the vinegar." So she sat beside the reapers, and he served her roasted grain, and she ate and was satisfied and had some left.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Boaz invites Ruth to his table — "eat of the bread and dip your piece in the vinegar." She eats, is satisfied, and has leftovers. The abundance and personal generosity of Boaz exceed the requirements of the gleaning law. Grace goes beyond what the law demands.

verses.flow: And Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, "May he be blessed of the LORD who has not withdrawn his kindness to the living and to the dead." And Naomi said to her, "The man is our close relative; he is one of our redeemers."
verses.note: HEBREW: "One of our redeemers" (migoaleinu) — first explicit use of go'el (kinsman-redeemer) in Ruth. The go'el had legal responsibilities: redeem sold property (Lev. 25:25), redeem a relative from slavery (Lev. 25:47-49), avenge blood (Num. 35:19), and marry a brother's widow (Deut. 25:5-10). Naomi now sees God's hand at work: chesed "to the living and to the dead."

verses.note: HEBREW: "Security" (manoach) — from the root nuach, meaning rest, settled security. Related to the "rest" (menuchah) Naomi prayed for in 1:9. Naomi is now actively working to secure the redemption she earlier despaired of.

verses.flow: And it shall be when he lies down, that you shall mark the place where he lies, and you shall go and uncover his feet and lie down; then he will tell you what you shall do."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Uncover his feet" (wegillit margelotav) — margelot is related to regel (foot). The act has symbolic significance: feet/sandals are connected to legal transactions of land and marriage (cf. 4:7-8; Deut. 25:9). This is not seduction but a legal claim upon the kinsman-redeemer.

words.g: to be the next of kin

verses.flow: And he said, "Who are you?" And she answered, "I am Ruth your maidservant. Spread your wing over your maidservant, for you are a redeemer."
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "Spread your wing (kanaf) over your maidservant, for you are a redeemer (go'el)" — Ruth directly echoes Boaz's own blessing from 2:12, where he spoke of God's wings (kanaf). She asks Boaz to be the human fulfillment of the divine protection he invoked. The go'el must be a kinsman (Boaz is), must be willing (Boaz is), and must be able to pay the price (Boaz can). Christ fulfills all three: He became our kinsman through incarnation (Heb. 2:14), was willing (John 10:18), and paid the full price (1 Pet. 1:18-19).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then he said, "May you be blessed of the LORD, my daughter. You have shown your last kindness to be better than the first by not going after young men, whether poor or rich.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Your last kindness" (chasdekh ha'acharon) — chesed again. Ruth's "first" chesed was loyalty to Naomi (1:16-17); her "last" is seeking the proper kinsman-redeemer rather than a younger man. She acts within the covenant structure rather than for personal advantage.

verses.flow: And now, my daughter, do not fear. All that you say I will do for you, for all the gate of my people knows that you are a woman of excellence.
verses.note: HEBREW: "A woman of excellence" ('eshet chayil) — the same phrase used in Prov. 31:10 ("an excellent wife"). Chayil parallels Boaz's own description as 'ish gibbor chayil (2:1). They are matched in character.

verses.flow: Remain this night, and when morning comes, if he will redeem you, good; let him redeem. But if he is not willing to redeem you, then I will redeem you, as the LORD lives. Lie down until the morning."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "As the LORD lives" (chay YHWH) — Boaz swears by the living God. His commitment to redeem is an oath, not merely a promise. The legal process must be followed — the nearer kinsman has first right — but Boaz commits himself irrevocably if that kinsman declines.

verses.flow: And he said, "Give me the cloak that is on you and hold it." So she held it, and he measured six measures of barley and laid it on her. Then he went into the city.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Six measures of barley" — the unit is unspecified (not an ephah, which would be impossibly heavy). The six measures may symbolize incompleteness (six, not seven), pointing to the fullness of redemption still to come in chapter 4.

verses.flow: Now Boaz went up to the gate and sat down there, and behold, the redeemer of whom Boaz had spoken was passing by. So he said, "Turn aside, friend, sit down here." And he turned aside and sat down.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Friend" (peloni 'almoni) — literally "so-and-so," a deliberate anonymity. The unnamed kinsman's refusal to redeem erases his name from memory, while Boaz's willingness to redeem makes his name eternal. The text enacts what it teaches: the one who will not redeem becomes nameless.

verses.flow: So I thought to inform you, saying, 'Buy it before those who are sitting here, and before the elders of my people. If you will redeem it, redeem it; but if not, tell me that I may know, for there is no one besides you to redeem it, and I am after you.' And he said, "I will redeem it."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The nearer kinsman says "I will redeem" — he is willing when only land is involved. The test comes in verse 5-6 when the full cost is revealed. Partial willingness to redeem is not redemption at all.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Boaz said, "On the day you buy the field from the hand of Naomi, you must also acquire Ruth the Moabitess, the widow of the dead, in order to raise up the name of the dead on his inheritance."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Raise up the name of the dead on his inheritance" (lehakim shem hammet 'al nachalato) — this combines the levirate duty (Deut. 25:5-6) with the land redemption law (Lev. 25:25). The redeemer must not only buy the land but marry the widow and produce an heir. The cost is total.

verses.flow: And the redeemer said, "I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I ruin my own inheritance. Redeem it for yourself; you may have my right of redemption, for I cannot redeem it."
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: The nearer kinsman cannot redeem — "lest I ruin my own inheritance" (pen 'ashchit 'et nachalati). He counts the cost and declines. This is the law's limitation: it can identify what must be done but cannot provide the power to do it. The nearer kinsman represents the law, which is "weak through the flesh" (Rom. 8:3). Boaz, the kinsman who can and will redeem at full cost, is the type of Christ. The law could not redeem us; Christ could and did.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Now this was the custom in former times in Israel concerning the redemption and the exchange of land: to confirm any matter, a man removed his sandal and gave it to another; and this was the manner of attestation in Israel.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Removed his sandal" (shalaf na'alo) — the sandal ceremony is attested in Deut. 25:9-10. The sandal represents the right of possession over land (cf. Ps. 60:8, "over Edom I shall throw My shoe"). Transferring the sandal transfers the redemption right.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: So the redeemer said to Boaz, "Buy it for yourself." And he removed his sandal.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then Boaz said to the elders and all the people, "You are witnesses today that I have bought from the hand of Naomi all that belonged to Elimelech and all that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon.

Moreover, Ruth the Moabitess, the widow of Mahlon, I have acquired as my wife in order to raise up the name of the dead on his inheritance, so that the name of the dead may not be cut off from his brothers or from the gate of his birthplace. You are witnesses today."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Boaz publicly acquires Ruth as his wife "to raise up the name of the dead." He does what the nearer kinsman would not. His public declaration before witnesses completes the legal redemption — land, widow, and name are all secured. The dead man's line will not be "cut off" (yikaret — the same root used for covenant cutting).

verses.flow: And all the people who were in the gate, and the elders, said, "We are witnesses. May the LORD make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and like Leah, both of whom built the house of Israel; and may you achieve wealth in Ephrathah and be famous in Bethlehem.
verses.note: HEBREW: The blessing of Rachel and Leah invokes the matriarchs who built all of Israel. The mention of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah (Gen. 38), is significant — Tamar, like Ruth, was a foreign woman whose irregular actions led to the messianic line. Both are named in Christ's genealogy (Matt. 1:3, 5).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Moreover, may your house be like the house of Perez whom Tamar bore to Judah, through the offspring which the LORD will give you by this young woman."
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "May your house be like the house of Perez" — the elders' blessing is prophetic. From Perez through Boaz and Ruth will come David, and from David, the Messiah. The genealogy at the end of Ruth (4:18-22) contains exactly ten names from Perez to David, a number of completeness.

verses.flow: So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife, and he went in to her. And the LORD enabled her to conceive, and she gave birth to a son.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The LORD enabled her to conceive" (wayyitten YHWH lah herayon) — after the deaths of Mahlon and Chilion, after ten barren years in Moab, God directly intervenes. The same God who "visited His people" with bread (1:6) now visits Ruth with a child. Divine sovereignty bookends the story.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Then the women said to Naomi, "Blessed is the LORD who has not left you without a redeemer today, and may his name be famous in Israel.
verses.note: HEBREW: "A redeemer" (go'el) — the women's blessing identifies the child Obed as a go'el for Naomi. The redeemer is not Boaz but the son born through Boaz's redemption. The chain of redemption extends: God -> Boaz -> Obed -> the messianic line.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: May he also be to you a restorer of life and a sustainer of your old age; for your daughter-in-law, who loves you and is better to you than seven sons, has given birth to him."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Better to you than seven sons" — seven is the number of completeness in Hebrew thought. Ruth surpasses the ideal. A Moabite daughter-in-law exceeds in covenant loyalty what the fullest measure of Israelite sons could provide. This overturns ethnic boundaries by chesed.

verses.flow: Then Naomi took the child and laid him in her lap, and became his nurse.
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "Naomi took the child and laid him in her lap (cheqah) and became his nurse ('omenet)" — Naomi, who was empty (1:21), is now full. She who was bitter (Mara) is restored. The child on her lap completes her redemption. Naomi's arc from fullness to emptiness to restoration is a type of Israel's own story: blessed, exiled, redeemed.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem plur

verses.flow: And the neighbor women gave him a name, saying, "A son has been born to Naomi!" So they named him Obed. He is the father of Jesse, the father of David.
verses.note: HEBREW: "A son has been born to Naomi" — legally and covenantally, Obed continues Elimelech's and Mahlon's line through Naomi. Obed ('oved) = "servant, worshiper." He is the father of Jesse (yishay), the father of David. From this threshing-floor encounter in Bethlehem comes Israel's greatest king, and through David, the Messiah (Matt. 1:5-6; Luke 3:32).

verses.flow: Now these are the generations of Perez: to Perez was born Hezron,
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "These are the generations of Perez" (we'elleh toledot Parets) — the toledot formula that structures Genesis (Gen. 2:4, 5:1, 6:9, etc.) appears here, linking Ruth back to the patriarchal narrative. The genealogy runs from Perez (Gen. 38, Judah's son by Tamar) through ten generations to David. This is the messianic line — Matt. 1:3-6 traces Christ's lineage through every name in this list. Ruth, the Moabite convert, stands in the direct ancestry of Jesus of Nazareth, the promised Messiah.

verses.flow: and to Hezron was born Ram, and to Ram was born Amminadab,
and to Amminadab was born Nahshon, and to Nahshon was born Salmon,
and to Salmon was born Boaz, and to Boaz was born Obed,
and to Obed was born Jesse, and to Jesse was born David.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The book ends with David's name — the narrative climax. Everything in Ruth leads to this: the famine, the sojourn, the deaths, the return, the gleaning, the threshing floor, the gate, the marriage, the birth. All of it is God's sovereign orchestration of the messianic line. Ruth entered as a Moabite widow; she exits as the great-grandmother of David, the anointed king from whose line the Christ would come (2 Sam. 7:12-16; Isa. 11:1; Matt. 1:1).
